note: End-to-end tests for the orchestrator: the user drops a photo in input/, the skill normalizes ANY format -> routes to category -> applies the measured recipe -> runs the Phase-A pipeline -> verifies + iterates against the source, collecting results in orchestrator/output/<name>/ and leaving input/ empty. The pipeline is real (Ink-Stitch vendored); a run is ~30s-2min and the iterate loop may run it several times. Fixtures live in evals/inputs/; a runner should copy the fixture into input/ before invoking. Baseline = no skill (agent still has the repo, CLAUDE.md and EMBROIDERY-PLAYBOOK.md).

evals.name: anyformat-heic-block-letters
evals.prompt: I dropped a phone photo in input/ (it's a HEIC): input/lets_celebrate.heic — it's the words 'LET'S CELEBRATE' in bold block capitals, black with a red accent. Turn it into an embroidery file I can stitch out, about 60mm tall.
evals.expected_output: Normalizes the HEIC (pillow-heif) to a PNG. Routes to LETTERS / block caps. Picks a size so strokes land ~2-4mm (~58-60mm here), small palette (2), snaps ink toward pure black + red. Runs the pipeline to orchestrator/output (or the eval output dir), gate PASS, then compares to the source and reports coverage. Artifacts: NAME_pro.vp3 + preview + threadlist. Honest note that typeset text is best built in Wilcom.

assertions.text: The HEIC was normalized to a raster PNG before tracing (no 'cannot open HEIC' failure)
Routed to the LETTERS category (not treated as a generic logo/photo)
Produced a NAME_pro.vp3 plus preview and threadlist in the output dir
Step-7 gate PASS
Thread match is exact/near-exact for black and red (switched to isacord when Madeira matched poorly)
Recognized and corrected the --lettering shatter (dropped it / used --purify-colors) so the letters read legibly; final source-coverage >= 85%
Stated the honest boundary that typeset text is best built in Wilcom lettering

evals.name: arabic-mixed-name
evals.prompt: I put a photo in input/ (input/ritaj-name.png) — please digitize it for a 140mm hoop. It's an Arabic name 'ريتاج' in big black calligraphy with a little illustration and a small subtitle line under it.
evals.expected_output: Routes the dominant subject to ARABIC/mixed. --purify-colors (NOT --lettering), --fill-method contour_fill for the sprawling script, small colour count, black snapped pure. Handles the mixed illustration by keeping palette small and sequencing back-to-front. Gate PASS or an explained near-miss; compares to source; notes the thin subtitle line may not survive at 140mm (min-width limit) and that connected cursive can't be all-satin from a trace.

assertions.text: Used --purify-colors and NOT --lettering (never shatters the cursive script)
Used --fill-method contour_fill for the sprawling calligraphy
Width set to ~140mm as requested
Step-7 gate PASS with areal density in the Arabic band (~0.4-0.8 st/mm^2)
The main name 'ريتاج' is present and legible in the preview
Compared to the source with the background-aware drift meter (did not report a bogus near-zero coverage from the tan background)
Stated the honest limits: sub-1.2mm subtitle may drop; connected cursive can't be all-satin from a trace

evals.name: anyformat-svg-icon-set
evals.prompt: I dropped a vector sheet of bright icons (a yellow star, a red heart, a blue star) in input/ as an SVG (input/icons.svg). Digitize it into a stitch file for a 150mm patch.
evals.expected_output: Rasterizes the SVG (cairosvg) big, routes to SIMPLE SHAPES. --thread-chart isacord (Madeira has no pure blue), --colors 3-4, --width-mm 150, no --purify-colors unless truly pure. Each shape one object by width, back-to-front. Gate PASS, source coverage ~99% (no shape dropped, star points not clipped), blue/red/yellow matched within reasonable dE. Overlay confirms all three icons present.

assertions.text: Rasterized the SVG to a large PNG before tracing (no 'cannot open SVG' failure)
Routed to SIMPLE SHAPES and used --thread-chart isacord
Width set to ~150mm; each of the three icons present (none dropped)
Step-7 gate PASS
Source coverage >= 95% (star points / heart tip not clipped)
Blue matched to a real blue cone (demonstrating why isacord beats Madeira here)
